name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَقَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ وَلَدًا ۗ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ ۖ بَل لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ كُلٌّ لَّهُۥ قَـٰنِتُونَ (١١٦)
```

```english
And they say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He! Rather, to Him belongs whatever there is in the heavens and the earth. All are obedient to Him, (116)
```

```arabic
بَدِيعُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَإِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (١١٧)
```

```english
the Originator of the heavens and the earth. When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is. (117)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقَالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَدًا** **They say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’** [Qarai: 2:116] The context indicates that those who made this claim were the Jews and the Christians; for the Jews said, "Ezra is the son of Allah," and the Christians said, "The Messiah is the son of Allah." Indeed, the discourse is directed toward the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب). The People of the Book initially uttered this statement—meaning their saying, "Allah has taken a son"—as a way of honoring their prophets, just as they said, "We are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones." Subsequently, it took on the guise of literal truth and reality. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, refuted them in these two verses, turning away from their claim by His saying: **بَل لَّهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ** **No, to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens...** [Qarai: 2:116].

This refutation comprises two proofs, each of which negates the possibility of birth or the existence of an offspring from Him, the Glorified. For taking a son (اتخاذ الولد) entails that a natural being (موجود طبيعي) separates some parts of its own existence and detaches them from itself, then, through gradual nurturing (تربية تدريجية), transforms that part into an individual of its own species, similar to itself. However, He, the Glorified, is transcendent above having a likeness (المثل); rather, everything in the heavens and the earth is His property (مملوك له), subsisting in its essence through Him (قائم الذات به), and is devoutly obedient (قانت)—abased in an existential humility (ذلة وجودية) before Him. How then could any thing be a son to Him, being a specific likeness (مماثلاً نوعياً) in relation to Him?

Furthermore, He, the Glorified, is the Originator (بديع) of the heavens and the earth; He only creates what He creates without a prior model (مثال سابق). Thus, nothing of His creation resembles a previous creation, nor does His act resemble the act of another in terms of imitation, likeness, or gradual progression (التدريج). He does not achieve results through intermediary causes (التوصل بالأسباب); rather, when He decrees a matter, He only says to it, "Be," and it is (كن فيكون), without a prior model or gradual process. How then can the "taking of a son" be attributed to Him, when its realization requires nurturing and gradual development?

Thus, His saying: **لَّهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ ۖ كُلٌّ لَّهُ قَانِتُونَ** **To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth; all are obedient to Him** [Qarai: 2:116] is a complete proof. And His saying: **بَدِيعُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ ۖ وَإِذَا قَضَىٰ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ** **Originator of the heavens and the earth! When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is** [Qarai: 2:116] is another complete proof. From these two verses, the following is derived:

**First:** The law of worship (العبادة) encompasses all created beings among those in the heavens and the earth.

**Second:** His act, the Exalted, is non-gradual (غير تدريجي). It is inferred from here that every gradual being (موجود تدريجي) possesses a non-gradual aspect through which it emanates from Him, the Exalted, as He said: **إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَن يَقُولَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ** **All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is** [Qarai: 36:82], and He said: **وَمَا أَمْرُنَا إِلَّا وَاحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ** **Our command is but a single word, like the twinkling of an eye** [Qarai: 54:50]. The detailed discussion of this Quranic reality will come, Allah willing, in the commentary on His saying: **إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا** **All His command, when He wills something...** [Qarai: 36:82], so await it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **سُبْحَانَهُ** **Immaculate is He!** [Qarai: 2:116] is a verbal noun (مصدر) meaning "glorification" (التسبيح). It is only used in the possessive construction (مضافاً) and functions as an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) for an omitted verb—that is, "I glorify Him with a glorification" (سبحته تسبيحاً). The verb was omitted, and the verbal noun was annexed to the object pronoun and placed in its stead. This word conveys a divine discipline (تأديب إلهي) of declaring transcendence (التنزيه) whenever something is mentioned that is unbecoming of His holy presence, the Exalted and Sanctified.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كُلٌّ لَّهُ قَانِتُونَ** **all are obedient to Him** [Qarai: 2:116]. Devout obedience (القنوت) is worship (العبادة) and self-abasement (التذلل).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **بَدِيعُ السَّمَاوَاتِ** **Originator of the heavens** [Qarai: 2:116]. The originality (بداعة) of a thing is its lack of resemblance to anything else that is known or familiar.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَيَكُونُ** **and it is** [Qarai: 2:116] is a resultative (تفريع) following the command "Be" (كن), and it is not in a conditional context that would require the jussive mood.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi* and *al-Basa'ir*, narrated from Sudayr al-Sayrafi, who said: I heard 'Imran ibn A'yan asking Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: **بَدِيعُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** (Originator of the heavens and the earth). Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: **إن الله عز وجل ابتدع الأشياء كلها بعلمه على غير مثال كان قبله ، فابتدع السموات والأرضين ولم يكن قبلهن سموات ولا أرضون أما تسمع لقوله : وكان عرشه على الماء ؟** — "Indeed, Allah, Mighty and Majestic, originated all things by His knowledge without any prior model. He originated the heavens and the earths when there were no heavens or earths before them. Have you not heard His saying: 'And His Throne was upon the water'?"

**I say:** In this narration, there is another subtle insight: that the "water" intended in His saying, the Exalted: **وَكَانَ عَرْشُهُ عَلَى الْمَاءِ** (And His Throne was upon the water) is something other than the physical instance (المصداق) of water that we have. This is evidenced by the fact that creation is established upon originality (البداعة), and the Divine Sovereignty (السلطنة الإلهية) was established and poised upon the "water" before the creation of these heavens and earths; thus, it is something other than [this] water. The completion of this discussion will come under His saying, the Exalted: **وَكَانَ عَرْشُهُ عَلَى الْمَاءِ** **and His Throne was upon the water** [Qarai: 11:7].

## (Scientific and Philosophical Discussion)

Experiments have demonstrated that any two existing things differ in their individual identities (الشخصيات), even if they are united in their universal concepts (الكليات). Even for two beings where the senses cannot distinguish the point of difference between them, the "armed sense" (microscopic or enhanced perception) perceives it. Philosophical proof (البرهان الفلسفي) also necessitates this; for if two hypothesized beings were not distinguished from one another by something external to their essence (الذات), the cause of the hypothesized multiplicity would not be external to their essences. In that case, the essence would be pure (صرفة) and unmixed, and a pure thing (صرف الشيء) does not double or repeat. Thus, what was hypothesized as multiple would be one and not multiple. Therefore, every existing thing is a being whose essence is distinct from any other being. Every existent is original in its existence (بديع الوجود), without a prior or familiar model. And Allah, the Glorified, is the Originator (المبتدع), the Originator of the heavens and the earth.
